item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The SCP feels too much like a J to me. The idea of a truly random event being a thaumiel class SCP is quite good, however I think the article falls short in the execution. The fact that whether someone takes a receipt or not seems a bit too silly and too common place to be an anomalous event. +1 for the idea, - 1 for the execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the Korean branch, I read an opinion such that: (translated by myself)

Isn't this SCP just a duplication of the Bistromathics in The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy? Aside of the difference with "receipt" and "bill", those two are quite similar, in the aspect of secret mathematical principles and random number generators.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you're misunderstanding the anomaly. Addressing your first numbered comments:

+ show block
– hide block

1: Whether something commonplace is considered part of baseline reality or part of nearly uncontainable keter anomaly is often up for debate in universe, and is pretty much down to writers preference in universe. It makes sense that this one is considered anomalous, though, I'll explain in a second.

2 and 3: The real life parallel lavarand is can't be predicted in our universe, but it can be predicted in the foundationverse:

For example, quantum mechanics, weather, ocean currents, and the algorithms for standard random number generators can be meaningfully predicted

This is the anomaly of 4559. It's not just that your choice of receipt or no receipt is unpredictable, it's that everything else up to and including quantum mechanics is predictable, when it shouldn't be. The containment procedures maintain normality by hiding this from the rest of the science world. Beyond that, there isn't much else to contain without removing the anomaly from existence entirely, which goes against foundation protocol.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn
(ah thanks zyn)

As for your collapsed comments, I will reply to those in my own collapsible lol

+ show block
– hide block

1. Beyond the fact that 4559 implies that nearly all understood randomness in the universe is actually predictable, and that for some reason receipt choice isn't, there isn't any other "danger" of anomaly to 4559. There isn't any way for a customer or employee involved in a 4559 occurrence to suddenly realize that the choice they made was any more random than they had perceived any other occurrence to be. There isn't anything to capture or monitor, and foundation has eyes in almost every security camera anyway for other reasons.

2. Not to beat the point to death but yea, stuff like quantum mechanics being unpredictable according to science is considered normalcy, and if any tests of 4559 in any regard return anything other than this fact, the foundation is to hide it.

3. Causing a massive shift in the public's actions for something they otherwise wouldn't notice sort seems like a lot of work for the foundation to achieve not much, since that form of interaction would still be the only random one regardless of whether someone performs it or not. Also, having a single location in foundation control would work, yeah, but 4559 is like a coinflip. It's a yes or no question, and limiting it to one location would drastically lower the speed at which random numbers can be generated. Imagine a line of D-Class fellas lining up to toss a coin vs 20,000 lines of people constantly flipping coins. The foundation uses these random results to contain other anomalies, so slowing that down would just cause problems elsewhere.

Also a headline like that would be a pretty funny information breach lol

oh edit: I was reading more of the comments and author says this:

Well, the implication was that this was set in a world where science has improved far enough where the world can predict weather, climate, atomic decay and all that shit, they can find what causes which outcome, even in weather, where you have to account for every single particle on earth…

but they can't find any underlying causes for each choice for whether a subject wants a receipt or not. That was sorta the punchline, ya know?

I hadn't considered this, but I guess that'd make it so that the fact that there is any randomness at all is anomalous. Both interpretations are pretty interesting, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this.

It feels like this could be developed into an -EX (can it??), considering 1. It is basically commonplace, 2. The containment protocol feels so mundane that it might as well not be contained, and 3. Has a real life parallel: Lavarand (actually used by Cloudflare apparently!), making it a potential lesson also digging into real life mechanical RNGs.

On point 2, more specifically that is the premise - "Given a choice for a receipt or not, as long as the choice is there, will be inexplicably random" - is not really being addressed by the current containment protocol.

+ omg I wrote way too much for this pls forgive me
– hide block

1. In the past, regarding anomalies that affect the whole area, it's pretty common for the Foundation to build complete sites surrounding the anomalies to contain them. Here the grocery stores involved appears not to be contained at all. I think it'd feel more appropriate - even if not necessary, just as a precaution - to assign some people monitoring SCP-4559? Does it make sense? If it's not captured on-site shouldn't someone be in charge of monitoring it? Is it just me?

2. According to the description, nothing seems to influence SCP-4559 and the chance for each choice is consistently unpredictable & symmetrical. This begs the question then, why target "scientific institutions and other organizations" to remove their data, considering it would be a reasonable conclusion that no matter how much they research and report on their findings, SCP-4559 itself would remain uninfluenced?

3. Is it just to keep SCP-4559 alive? Would a few headlines like "Free-will under threat; Can you really choose whether to get a receipt?" suddenly stop every store from handing out receipts? If the goal is just to use SCP-4559, isn't it far more economical to only have one (1) store rigged instead of maintaining connections with nearly 20,000 stores? Now that I think about it, why does it even matter that we keep it alive to use it? The analysis claims,

In the case where there are either rules that state that the customer always receives the receipt, or in the event that the customer is given the receipt without their prior approval, SCP-4559 will not trigger.

This leads me to believe that SCP-4559 can and will trigger as long as the store does not rule on whether a receipt must be given and the receipt isn't delivered before the choice, how about we (the Foundation) just set up our own store fulfilling that criteria (simple?) and run the RNG with any personnel on demand when it's needed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get it, but I never know how to feel about these kinds of articles. "Joke but not an actual -J article" is pretty well-trodden ground, and the conceptual conceit here *is* funny, it just doesn't really give me any strong feelings. I do appreciate the brevity.

For what it's worth, true randomness (such as it is) is kind of a slippery concept to even qualify. There are real random number generators that use things like blob positions in lava lamps to pull their integers and work well enough.

Conversely I think people downvoting it for being too silly or not scientifically accurate (on The SCP Wiki? noooo, say it ain't so) are overreacting.

Neutral for now from me I guess? It kind of feels like a Series 1 article, for good and ill.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would upvote if the object was a joke, but not a freaking thaumiel. Sorry, but it's a downvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for the idea, though I agree with many of the other comments stating that the article would benefit from more technical/clinical language.
Paper or plastic?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, I realized that. Thanks for reading, either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the users' problem comes from the fact that you talk about quantum mechanics. I'm by no means a physicist (not even close), but I'm fairly sure that a key part of quantum mechanics is that it's probabilistic. It's not that we don't have instruments powerful enough to predict the outcome — it's just impossible on a fundamental level.

I can buy into it if the argument is that the universe is purely deterministic. This brings in the idea that free will exclusively hinges on the fact that people don't know if they want a receipt or not. Neither of these themes are explored in the article, which kind of leaves me underwhelmed.

As it stands, the concept is pretty interesting but ultimately not very well explored. -1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No. This is very disgusting scientific illiteracy.

+ show block
– hide block

Well, the implication was that this was set in a world where science has improved far enough where the world can predict weather, climate, atomic decay and all that shit, they can find what causes which outcome, even in weather, where you have to account for every single particle on earth…

but they can't find any underlying causes for each choice for whether a subject wants a receipt or not. That was sorta the punchline, ya know?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For example, quantum mechanics, weather, ocean currents … can be meaningfully predicted

No. This is very disgusting scientific illiteracy. I regret what i cannot downvote it many times.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How was SCP-4559 discovered? How was its anomalousness proven? How it's useful enough to be Thaumiel?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but I have a minor nitpick: Thaumiel SCPs are classified as hell. I would put a mention of the file requiring 5/4559 access to see. But I get if you don't want to do that for pacing reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry, but this wasn't my cup of tea. The anomaly could definitely be described better; I had to read through 2 or 3 times before I could accurately visualize what was going on. In the end, I didn't find it that compelling.

However, my least favorite part was the whole "randomness" part.

Currently, SCP-4559 is the only truly unpredictable or "random" phenomenon in the universe known to the Foundation. For example, quantum mechanics, weather, ocean currents, and the algorithms for standard random number generators can be meaningfully predicted, but SCP-4559 cannot be predicted in any meaningful way.

Although all of these things could be predicted, it's nigh impossible. Being able to accurately predict weather requires a near total understanding of the system, as well as a good accumulation of past weather data. And even then, my local weather stations gets it wrong 25% of the time, and any new stimuli introduced to a system can easily change it in total.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find this more disturbing than humorous.

+1
I'm very surprised by how well this was received, thank you all!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. Sounds about right… I alwaks knew my anxiety when recieving a receipt was an anomaly!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno man, it's kinda passing my suspension of disbelief, because if working retail for ten years taught me anything, the quickest way to guarantee a customer will want a receipt is not to print one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I adore any SCP that can be humorous while also being a non J. I like Js as well, but this type of balance between joke and actual SCP is wonderful. Great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's some of the stuff running through my head:

  • Can we influence it with public notices encouraging customers to 'go paperless'?
  • What if I tell Group A to always pick the receipt for a month? Then I tell them they're free to choose again. Does this condition them?
  • What SCPs require randomness that supersedes omniscience? I'd like a cross link if you know any.
  • Containment procedures based on preserving optional receipts would be really cool. Subsidies to paper mills making receipts. Backing of consumer protection laws. Suppression of online grocery shopping. Protocols to minimise the use of food rationing in emergency scenarios. Protocols to preserve consumerism in general.
  • What if the SCP deems one of those measures to be cheating and suddenly an entire country becomes non-random until interference is removed?
  • This is fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with most of the criticisms made above. The idea of whether or not a person wants a receipt being the only "truly random" event in the whole universe just seems a little too jokey for its own good. Like, haha, yes, everyone can relate because in real life everyone basically answers this question randomly when we're at a store. But that joke is the entire conceit of the article, and I just don't think it's enough. I think an event that is really, truly, anomalously random is an interesting seed of an idea, but it would need a much more rigorous treatment than this article gives.

I also agree that some of the writing seems un-clinical- maybe it's just me, but phrases like "studies that revolve around" and "truly random thing" don't read like something that would appear in a scientific document. Due to these issues, I had to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree that the idea seems a bit silly, like something I'd almost expect from a -J.

The only known truly random event in the world is… the customer's choice of receipt? And this is apparently different enough from advanced random number generators that it's classified as Thaumiel and used to contain SCPs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I largely agree with the above. There are several places where the wording is repetitive or not as clinical as I think it should be. In a short work, each word matters, and I feel there are several areas where the word choice is not ideal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short, sweet, and simple. Yet good. A primary example to how good articles don't have to be long.

(Also, could this be at least partially inspired by either the Improbability or Bistromathics drive from the H2G2 series?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of a truly random event (and the effectiveness it could have for things such as cryptography) is a cool idea. You appropriately make it a very short article, too.

Some things holding me back from an upvote:

+ show block
– hide block
  • Making it whether or not someone wants a receipt feels a little too silly. Maybe that's just me, though. I was hoping for something similarly trivial, but still (in some respect) mechanical (for example, the idea of the Foundation's cryptography relying on a specific register at a specific store and whether or not it rounds up or down when calculating taxes has a much more "fun" and whimsical energy to me). Take this with prescription grade salt, though.
  • The real kicker is the prose itself. It feels very much like a more technical approach would work wonders, here; instead of saying "truly random", just "an outcome that cannot be predicted" (without mentioning quantum computers, etc). The phrase "truly random" was particularly egregious, to me; I feel "impossible to predict" or another more technical term would be applicable, here.
  • And the final kicker: True random number generators actually do exist. Not truly true, but so genuinely random that they are all but literally impossible to predict. I suppose this is addressed by the fact that in this setting, it's theoretically possible to predict those things, but it's also theoretically possible to predict this thing (the receipts). Because, y'know — anomalies.
  • One final note — I feel like you missed an opportunity here to make this about the choice between paper and plastic, and to then have the Foundation force grocery stores to keep both so they don't break their random number generator.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is probably the most Douglas Adams-y thing i've ever read on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is what we really need out of the Thaumiel class. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This ia so damn simple but I love it :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because this is a Thaumiel SCP I think the Foundation would focus on it more than say, a safe SCP,
I would suggest adding either test logs or interviews.

Nonetheless I enjoyed it, +1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
SCP-4559 cannot be predicted in any meaningful way.

I had an idea and some people told me it was good so I crapped this out. It's a boldpost and my first thaumiel. Anxious to see how it does, so let's see for ourselves.

Art

A collection of SCP art, including SCP-4559
by Mr-Firework
